ops.title: A \"total\" exception boundary is only total if its own error-reporting (str/repr/log/record-build) cannot raise
ops.body:
When you widen an exception handler into a TOTAL boundary — an `except (Exception, get_cancelled_exc_class())` that must let nothing escape, e.g. a `finally` epilogue that must never replace the propagating exception — the boundary's OWN recovery and reporting code is itself an unguarded call site. `reason=str(exc)` / `repr(exc)`, logging the caught object, or building an abort record can each raise (a broken `__str__`, a re-entrant format, a failing serializer) and re-break the very totality the widening was for.

**Why:** shipped as a real bug in athome. A3.11 widened the research-loop epilogue's `except` to catch anything and route it to the abort latch, but kept `reason=str(exc)` inside that handler; a caught exception with a broken `__str__` raised there, escaped the `finally`, suppressed the latch, and replaced the original `CancelledError` — the exact "nothing escapes" property the round existed to guarantee. The round that ADDED the total boundary is precisely where the unguarded formatting slipped through. Tracked as cc-notes 389b4ff (A3.12).

**How to apply:** when making a boundary total, treat every call inside it — str/repr of the caught exception, logging, record construction — as fallible. Do the durable write (latch/log) with a fixed or already-safe reason BEFORE any stringification that could throw, or guard the coercion; and add a broken-`__str__` (and broken-`__repr__`) identity test asserting the original exception still propagates and the durable record is present. Same totality discipline the numeric validators got (finite/coerce never raises) — apply it to the boundary's reporting path, not just its inputs. See [[experiment-at-home-shared-infra]].
